stem: During inference, compared to training, a neural network:

options.text: Updates its weights based on prediction errors
Uses more memory because it stores gradients
Uses frozen weights to make predictions without updating them
Requires the same compute as training

explanation_after: During inference, weights are fixed (frozen). The model only does a forward pass to compute predictions. No gradients are computed or stored, making it much cheaper than training.

stem: Why does training typically require much more compute than inference?

options.text: Training uses a larger dataset
Training requires backpropagation to compute and store gradients through the entire network
Training runs on CPU while inference uses GPU
Training requires more RAM

explanation_after: Training requires a forward pass AND a backward pass (backpropagation) to compute gradients for every parameter. Gradients must be stored for the update step. This is 2-3x more compute than a single forward pass.

stem: A company trains a model once and then serves it to millions of users daily. Their cost is dominated by:

options.text: Training cost, because it happens every day
Inference cost, because it happens billions of times
Data storage, because datasets are large
Training and inference cost equally

explanation_after: Training happens once (or infrequently). Inference happens every time a user makes a request — millions of times per day. At scale, inference cost dwarfs training cost.

options.text: Evaluates the model accuracy on a test set
Switches the model to inference mode, disabling Dropout and using running stats for BatchNorm
Exports the model to a file
Freezes the model weights permanently

explanation_after: `model.eval()` sets the model to inference mode: Dropout is disabled (all neurons active), and BatchNorm uses stored running statistics instead of batch statistics. Forgetting this causes inconsistent predictions.

stem: Which technique reduces inference latency by representing weights with fewer bits?

options.text: Data augmentation
Dropout
Quantization
Early stopping

explanation_after: Quantization reduces model precision from float32 to int8 or int4, shrinking model size and speeding up inference with minimal accuracy loss. Widely used to deploy large models on phones and edge devices.

options.text: Training a model on two datasets simultaneously
Using the weights of a pre-trained model as starting point for a new task, rather than starting from random weights
Transferring data from one format to another
Copying architecture but not weights from another model

explanation_after: Transfer learning uses a model's learned representations (weights) from one task as an initialisation for another. Features learned on ImageNet (edges, textures, shapes) transfer to many vision tasks, reducing required data and training time.
